text.content: An analysis of the determinants of students’ performance in e-learning

title.plain_text: An analysis of the determinants of students’ performance in e-learning

tables.heading: Conclusions
rows: 3 courses: Introduction to Financial Accounting, Introduction to Mathematics and Principles of Microeconomics

[Sex, Age, Schooling, Work Experience, Experience at UOC, Grade Average, Ability, Time Spent Studying Relevant Bibliography, Time Spent Studying Non-Relevant Bibliography, Motivation Perception, ]Feedback Intensity, ICT-Based Methodology, ICT uses, Motivation]
Invalid responses and non-respondents were disconsidered. Data was reduced from 850 students to 127.

Motivation is the most important predictor of performance and the only variable that is significant at 0.01 significance. 

Students who are older and are good on ICXT technologies arre more confident about their ability to succeed.

SEM is a good model to explain predictors of student performance. 

Motivation is one of the better predictors of student success in online learning.

Schooling and Feedback intensity were considered as unimportant factors to the SEM. 

Motivation had the highest standardized coefficient (0.298). 

Age and Semesters also indirectly affect student performance.

ICT and teaching methods also do not seem to have impact on student performance. Likely due to the teaching methods across the 3 courses being somewhat similar.

Questionnaire data

Courses used are teached in homogenous way → hence no differences between teaching methods and ICT usage could be found.

Does not consider university organizational structure of the universities.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Variability in teaching methods and learning styles has effects in the potential positive effects of technology adoption in student performance. The paper presents an experiment setup based on SEM. Motivation is the main variable to determine success in the context of online learning.

text.content: Variability in teaching methods and learning styles has effects in the potential positive effects of technology adoption in student performance. The paper presents an experiment setup based on SEM. Motivation is the main variable to determine success in the context of online learning.

blocks.text: ICT infrastructure aims to improve student performance and achievement. The paper attempts to answer 2 main questions:

text.content: ICT infrastructure aims to improve student performance and achievement. The paper attempts to answer 2 main questions:

rich_text.plain_text: ICT infrastructure aims to improve student performance and achievement. The paper attempts to answer 2 main questions:

blocks.text: ICT has shifted education considerably. It creates new opportunities for better communication between teachers and students, it also forces new interactions with educational resources. The potential is recognized for scale factors: improve the performance and achievement of more students with the same or even fewer resources. However, it is not self-evident that making the investment is enough. Institutions require specific adaptations to the way courses are delivered in order to make full use of the digital evolution.

blocks.text: Some research shows that e-learning students actually perform worse than their face-to-face counterparts. These works often fail to distinguish between teaching methods or learning styles, instead opting to treat online learning as an homogenous entity. Out of those works, there are some interesting observations that explain this difference:

blocks.text: Other works show that adjusting teaching methods to the digital tools actually has positive effects in student performance. Taking into account the appearance of active teaching methods it is possible to adapt teaching methodologies to become more student-focused and simultaneously make full use of ICT.

blocks.text: Thus, it becomes essencial to identify key variables that explain the effects of technology adoption in student performance. E-learning enviroments are a good playground to implement active teaching methods. Yet, there is a problem of resistance by current professors who look at the method as inneficient at best and inefective at worst. Also, the fact that a tool is available does not necessarily mean that it will be used by the students.

blocks.text: The question for the current paradigm is not should we use technology. Instead it should be how to use technology at an institutional, teacher and student level.

blocks.text: The data was collected via questionnaire. The data was plugged into a created SEM and an analysis of the explanatory variables was performed. Motivation has the highest standardized coefficient (0.298), meaning that it was found to be the most important variable of the model.

blocks.text: Some variables expected to be relevant such as Schooling and Feedback Intensity were not found to ave an effect on student performance.  Conversely, motivation was found to be the most important feature.

blocks.text: Conclusions

text.content: Conclusions

rich_text.plain_text: Conclusions

blocks.text:
Motivation is the most important driving force for student ability to pass exams and, according to this model, is actually the only variable with a positive and significant effect on student performance. Motivation is positively influenced by self-perception of efficiency. 

Other variables have indirect positive effects:

blocks.text:
It is possible that these variables affect perception of efficiency which, in turn, has positive effects on motivation and consequently Student Performance. Older students tend to be more confident but, simultaneously, tend to be more experienced when it comes to working and having better pre-knowledge.

However, somewhat surprisingly, the authors find no relationship between pre-knowledge and performance. Also, variables measuring student effort also do not seem to impact student performance.
